summary: DeVault announces writefreesoftware.org, a new resource he created to provide an accessible introduction to free software philosophy and practical licensing guidance. He frames it as a response to the FSF's messaging failures, which he previously criticized for being incomprehensible and inaccessible to newcomers. The site aims to be independent of any particular organization and cooperative with the open source movement. He positions it as a practical step toward moving the free software movement forward where the FSF has stagnated.
key_points: The FSF's websites (fsf.org, gnu.org) are incomprehensible and inaccessible to newcomers trying to learn about free software
writefreesoftware.org provides accessible free software philosophy and practical licensing advice as an alternative to GNU's resources
The site deliberately has no association with any particular organization and no policy of non-cooperation with open source
The resource is itself free software (CC-BY-SA 4.0) hosted on SourceHut, inviting community contributions
money_quotes: fsf.org and gnu.org together suffer from no small degree of incomprehensibility and inaccessibility which makes it difficult for new participants to learn about the movement and apply it in practice to their own projects.
This is something which is relatively easily fixed!
No particular association with any particular free software project or organization
No policy of non-cooperation with the open source movement
This resource is not going to solve all of the FSF's problems, but it is an easy way to start putting the effort in to move the free software movement forward.
key_insight: Rather than waiting for the FSF to fix its messaging problems, individual advocates can build better, more accessible free software resources independently.
